tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Neoadjuvant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinically positive nodes)

trial_data.efficacy: 12-month EFS: 83.7% vs. 57.2% with adjuvant nivolumab (P < .01). MPR rate: 59%. OS data not mature.
reference_numbers: NEOSYS 1-7 (Blank CU, N Engl J Med 2024;391:1696-1708)

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is category 1 only for initial presentation of stage III disease with clinically positive nodes that have not received prior targeted or immune therapy [NEOSYS-1]. Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg is generally preferred over ipilimumab 3 mg/kg + nivolumab 1 mg/kg, as it is associated with similar pathologic response and RFS rates and lower toxicities [NEOSYS-1]. MPR following 2 doses is associated with >90% 3-year RFS with no additional adjuvant therapy; optimal adjuvant therapy is not clear but can include anti-PD-1 monotherapy or observation for MPR, or anti-PD-1 or dabrafenib/trametinib for those lacking MPR [NEOSYS-1]. For low-volume in-transit disease, the high risk of toxicities associated with combination regimens may outweigh the benefit [NEOSYS-1].

reference_numbers: Blank CU, N Engl J Med 2024;391:1696-1708
Tetzlaff MT, et al. Ann Oncol 2018;29:1861-1868

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (Neoadjuvant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: 2-year EFS: 72% vs. 49% with adjuvant-only pembrolizumab (P < .01). MPR rate: 53%. OS data not mature.
reference_numbers: NEOSYS 5 (Patel SP, N Engl J Med 2023;388:813-823)

tier_1_regimens.notes: It is unclear which neoadjuvant regimen is more active since they have not been directly compared [NEOSYS-1]. The SWOG S1801 trial assigned patients to complete a total of one year of systemic pembrolizumab irrespective of pathologic response status; withholding adjuvant therapy following MPR was NOT tested in this trial and is not routinely recommended [NEOSYS-6]. A 21% pCR rate was reported after 3 doses of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab [NEOSYS-4].

reference_numbers: Patel SP, N Engl J Med 2023;388:813-823

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (Neoadjuvant — Other Recommended)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: Activity demonstrated but not studied in larger randomized studies with modern comparators.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Studies have demonstrated efficacy for nivolumab neoadjuvant monotherapy (1–3 doses every 2 weeks); however, these have not been studied in larger randomized studies, with modern comparators, or against active adjuvant therapy [NEOSYS-5].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab and Relatlimab-rmbw (Neoadjuvant — Other Recommended)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: Activity demonstrated; not studied in larger randomized studies with modern comparators.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Studies have demonstrated efficacy for nivolumab and relatlimab (2 doses every 4 weeks); however, these have not been studied in larger randomized studies, with modern comparators, or against active adjuvant therapy [NEOSYS-5]. In one small study, adjuvant nivolumab/relatlimab-rmbw was given for a total of 1 year of therapy. Adjustment based on pathologic response status has not been studied [NEOSYS-6].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (Neoadjuvant — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: Active in neoadjuvant setting for 4–12 weeks of preoperative therapy.
reference_numbers: NEOSYS 5 (Amaria RN, Lancet Oncol 2018;19:181-193; Long GV, Lancet Oncol 2019;20:961-971)

tier_1_regimens.notes: If immunotherapy is contraindicated, dabrafenib and trametinib could be considered for a short course (4–12 weeks) of preoperative therapy; however, this approach has not been studied in comparison with adjuvant dabrafenib and trametinib [NEOSYS-1, NEOSYS-5]. Other BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations can be considered in the event of unacceptable toxicities to dabrafenib/trametinib or based on side effect profiles [NEOSYS-1].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Adjuvant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then nivolumab maintenance

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Useful in Certain Circumstances (for oligometastatic resected stage IV)

trial_data.efficacy: Ipilimumab 3 mg/kg + nivolumab 1 mg/kg improved RFS compared with nivolumab or ipilimumab alone for resected stage IV melanoma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab + nivolumab combination did not improve EFS compared to nivolumab in resected stage III/IV melanoma with one possible exception: in the IMMUNED randomized phase II study of adjuvant therapy for resected stage IV melanoma, ipilimumab 3 mg/kg and nivolumab 1 mg/kg improved RFS compared with nivolumab or ipilimumab alone [ADJSYS 2]. For patients who have previously received systemic therapy for cutaneous melanoma, selection of adjuvant treatment regimen should be informed by response to prior systemic therapies [ADJSYS-1].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (Adjuvant — Preferred)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable

trial_data.efficacy: 1-year RFS: 71% vs. 61% (nivolumab vs. ipilimumab); HR = 0.65 (95% CI: 0.51–0.83; P < .001). 1-year DMFS: 80% vs. 73%; HR = 0.73 (95% CI: 0.55–0.95). Grade 3–4 AEs: 25% vs. 55%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for patients with AJCC 7th Edition stage IIIB/C disease. Category 2A for other stage III subgroups and satellite/in-transit disease. Nivolumab has shown a clinically significant improvement in RFS compared to high-dose ipilimumab [ADJSYS-1]. CheckMate 238 included 155 patients with in-transit melanoma only; nivolumab showed improvement in RFS compared to high-dose ipilimumab. The NCCN Panel agrees it is appropriate to extend the indication for adjuvant anti-PD-1 therapy to patients with clinical or macroscopic satellite/in-transit disease [ADJSYS-1]. Duration: 12 months.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (Adjuvant — Preferred)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for patients with clinically detected nodal metastases. For patients with clinically occult nodal disease, category 1 is limited to stage IIIA with at least one nodal metastasis >1 mm or stage IIIB/C per AJCC 7th Edition staging. Patients with in-transit metastases were excluded, so category 2A in this setting [ADJSYS-1]. Pembrolizumab has shown a clinically significant improvement in RFS compared to placebo, but its impact on OS has not yet been reported [ADJSYS-1]. Duration: 12 months.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (Adjuvant — Preferred for BRAF V600 Mutant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III resectable
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for stage III clinically positive nodes. For occult nodal disease, category 1 limited to stage IIIA with ≥1 mm nodal metastasis or stage IIIB/C per AJCC 7th Edition. If BRAF V600 mutation positive, other BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations can be considered in the event of unacceptable toxicities to dabrafenib/trametinib or based on side effect profiles [ADJSYS-1]. BRAF V600K mutated metastatic melanoma may have slightly lower response/benefit than V600E. Duration: 12 months.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab (Adjuvant — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable with prior anti-PD-1 exposure

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then every 12 weeks for up to 3 years

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab is recommended only if the patient has prior exposure to anti-PD-1 therapy. The recommended dose is 3 mg/kg (extrapolated from ECOG 1609 preliminary data suggesting similar efficacy with lower toxicity) [ADJSYS-1]. In EORTC 18071 using 10 mg/kg, grade 3–4 AEs were 54% and fatal AEs occurred in 1% of patients. In situations where adjuvant ipilimumab may be an option (eg, patients who progress during anti-PD-1 therapy with resectable disease), the recommended dose is 3 mg/kg [ADJSYS-1].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Preferred)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W maintenance

trial_data.efficacy: 4-year OS: 53% (nivo/ipi) vs. 46% (nivo) vs. 30% (ipi). ORR: 58% vs. 45% vs. 19%. Median PFS: 11.5 vs. 6.9 vs. 2.9 months. Grade 3–5 AEs: 59% vs. 22% vs. 28%. Grade 3–5 irAEs: 54% vs. 18%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred first-line option for unresectable or metastatic melanoma. Nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg has demonstrated clinically meaningful intracranial activity [MELSYS-3]. Considerations include higher efficacy but higher toxicity compared to anti-PD-1 monotherapy. Treatment duration: until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity [MELSYS-1]. Combination immune checkpoint blockade is associated with improved response rate, PFS, and OS compared with anti-PD-1 monotherapy [MELSYS-3].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab and Relatlimab-rmbw (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Preferred)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

trial_data.efficacy: Improved PFS vs. nivolumab alone. Median PFS: 10.1 vs. 4.6 months; HR = 0.75 (95% CI: 0.62–0.92).

tier_1_regimens.notes: First-line preferred option. Nivolumab and relatlimab-rmbw showed a 9%–12% ORR in patients with PD-1/PD-L1 refractory disease, supporting its role in later lines as well [MELSYS-3]. Treatment continues until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Preferred Monotherapy)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

trial_data.efficacy: CheckMate 066: ORR 43% vs. 14% (DTIC); median PFS 5.1 vs. 2.2 months (P < .001); median OS 37.5 vs. 11.2 months (P < .001). CheckMate 067: 4-year OS 46%. Long-term survival up to ~40%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Both nivolumab 240 mg Q2W and 480 mg Q4W are FDA-approved flat doses. Treatment duration: until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Responses are very durable, with many persisting after discontinuation. Anti-PD-1 therapy should be effective regardless of BRAF mutation status [MELSYS-3]. Subgroup analyses in CheckMate 067 showed similar efficacy regardless of PD-L1 expression, but descriptive analyses suggest patients with low PD-L1 may benefit more from combination therapy [MELSYS-4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Preferred Monotherapy)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 37% vs. 13% (P < .001). 2-year PFS: 31% vs. 14% (P < .0001). 2-year OS: 55% vs. 43% (P = .0008). Grade 3–5 treatment-related AEs: 17% vs. 20%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Results of KEYNOTE-006 support pembrolizumab as first-line therapy. Long-term follow-up showed that pembrolizumab provides long-term improvement in PFS and OS compared with ipilimumab monotherapy [MELSYS-1]. Median time to response approximately 3 months. Late responses can be observed more than a year after start of treatment. Most patients who achieve CR/PR and discontinue after 2 years maintain response with 2 years of follow-up [MELSYS-1].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Other Recommended for BRAF V600 Mutant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF V600 mutant

trial_data.n_patients: COMBI-d: 423 (211 dab/tram, 212 dab); COMBI-v: 704 (352 dab/tram, 352 vem)
trial_data.efficacy: COMBI-d: ORR 69% vs. 53% (P = .0014); median PFS 11.0 vs. 8.8 months (P = .0004); median OS 25.1 vs. 18.7 months (P = .0107). COMBI-v: ORR 64% vs. 51% (P < .001); median PFS 11.4 vs. 7.3 months (P < .001); OS: HR = 0.66 (P = .005). Grade 3–4 AEs: 48–52%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy has been shown to have superior response rate, PFS, and OS compared with BRAF inhibitor monotherapy, as well as a similar or better toxicity profile. The DREAMseq trial (ECOG-ACRIN EA6134) demonstrated that combination dabrafenib/trametinib versus combination nivolumab/ipilimumab showed superior outcomes with immunotherapy first-line [MELSYS-4]. BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy is considered appropriate for patients whose tumor harbors a BRAF mutation and who would benefit from a more rapid response [MELSYS-4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Encorafenib/Binimetinib (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Other Recommended for BRAF V600 Mutant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF V600 mutant

trial_data.efficacy: Encorafenib/binimetinib vs. vemurafenib: ORR 64% vs. 41%; median PFS 14.9 vs. 7.3 months (P < .0001); median OS 33.6 vs. 16.9 months (P < .0001). Grade 3–4 AEs: 64%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: In the COLUMBUS trial, encorafenib/binimetinib improved PFS and OS compared with vemurafenib monotherapy. Encorafenib/binimetinib vs. encorafenib monotherapy did not reach statistical significance for PFS (P = .050) or OS (P = .12) [MELSYS 1]. There are few data to inform selection among the BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy options as none have been directly compared [MELSYS-4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Other Recommended for BRAF V600 Mutant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF V600 mutant

drugs.name: Cobimetinib
drugs.schedule: Once daily for 21 days on/7 days off

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 70% vs. 50% (P < .0001); median PFS: 12.3 vs. 7.2 months (P < .0001); median OS: 22.3 vs. 17.4 months (P = .005). Grade 3–4 AEs: 75%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: The combination showed improved PFS and OS compared to vemurafenib monotherapy. Diarrhea was a prominent toxicity in the combination arm, particularly during the first cycle [MELSYS-1]. There are few data to inform selection among the available BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations [MELSYS-4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab (Second-Line/Subsequent Metastatic/Unresectable — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: CA184-002: ORR 11% (ipi) vs. 2% (gp100); median OS 10.1 vs. 6.4 months (P < .001). CA184-169: 3 mg/kg ORR 12%, median OS 11.5 months; 10 mg/kg ORR 15%, median OS 15.7 months (P = .04).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Although CheckMate 067 showed ipilimumab to have inferior outcomes compared to nivolumab/ipilimumab and nivolumab monotherapy in previously untreated patients, it is unclear whether the same would be true in patients who have progressed on prior systemic therapy, particularly anti-PD-1 therapy. For this reason, ipilimumab is included among acceptable options for previously treated patients [MELSYS-2]. Extended follow-up showed ipilimumab results in long-term survival in approximately 20% of patients [MELSYS-1].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Second-Line/Subsequent Metastatic/Unresectable — Preferred)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Combination produced approximately 28% ORR vs. 8% for ipilimumab alone. 6-month PFS: 35% vs. 13%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: A 94-patient trial randomized patients to ipilimumab and nivolumab versus ipilimumab alone following progression on anti-PD-1 therapy. The combination was associated with higher response rates and PFS [MELSYS-3]. This regimen is particularly useful for patients who progressed on prior anti-PD-1 monotherapy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocyte (TIL) Therapy — Lifileucel
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

drugs.name: Lifileucel
drugs.schedule: One-time infusion following lymphodepleting chemotherapy and high-dose IL-2

trial_data.efficacy: Durable response rates in anti-PD-1 refractory melanoma. For patients with good performance status previously treated with anti-PD-1-based therapy and BRAF/MEK inhibition, TIL therapy should be considered.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with good performance status who have been previously treated with anti-PD-1-based therapy and BRAF/MEK inhibition (if BRAF V600 mutation present), TIL therapy (with high-dose IL-2) should be considered, based on durable response rates in anti-PD-1 refractory melanoma [MELSYS-3]. TIL therapy currently requires a resectable metastasis for TIL harvesting and includes the use of nonmyeloablative chemotherapy and high-dose IL-2. If cellular therapy will be part of treatment planning, early referral to a center with melanoma expertise should be strongly considered following anti-PD-1 progression [MELSYS-3].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Low-Dose Ipilimumab + Pembrolizumab (Second-Line/Subsequent — Preferred for Progression Following Anti-PD-1)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated with anti-PD-1

trial_data.efficacy: Long-term follow-up shows manageable safety and durable responses. Pembrolizumab plus ipilimumab following anti-PD-1/L1 failure produced approximately 25%–33% response rates, often durable.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Recommended for patients with disease progression following anti-PD-1 therapy. The combination of ipilimumab/anti-PD-1 produces approximately 25%–33% response rates, often durable [MELSYS-3].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Activity in patients with confirmed progression on a PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor given as monotherapy or in combination.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Listed as an option for patients who have progressed on anti-PD-1-based therapy and BRAF/MEK inhibitors. This combination is listed as useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy [MELSYS-2].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: T-VEC + Ipilimumab (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for Injectable Metastases)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 39% vs. 18% (P = .002). Median PFS: 8.2 vs. 6.4 months (P = .35). OS: not significantly different.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B recommendation. Because results did not demonstrate improved PFS or OS, this is listed only as an option for second or subsequent-line therapy and is not a preferred option. The NCCN Panel voted not to include combination therapy with T-VEC plus systemic anti-PD-1 therapy as a recommended option due to insufficient randomized trial data on this specific combination [MELSYS-3].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Imatinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for KIT Mutant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, KIT mutation positive

trial_data.efficacy: 17%–30% ORR and 35%–57% disease control rate in patients with KIT-mutated or KIT-amplified metastatic melanomas. Most responses were of limited duration.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Activating KIT mutations are rare in cutaneous melanoma but more common in mucosal and acral subtypes. Imatinib is listed as useful in certain circumstances, not as a preferred agent, even for patients with qualifying mutations, because more effective therapies (immune checkpoint inhibitors, BRAF-targeted therapy) should be tried first [MELSYS-3]. Patient selection by molecular screening is essential.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Binimetinib Monotherapy (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for NRAS Mutant)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, NRAS Q61 mutation positive

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 15%; median PFS: 2.8 months. Binimetinib demonstrated a modest improvement in PFS with no improvement in OS compared with single-agent dacarbazine in NRAS-mutant melanoma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Recommended for progression following immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in NRAS mutation positive patients. The ORR and PFS are poor compared to BRAF inhibitors tested in other trials, so this is listed as useful in certain circumstances only [MELSYS-2, MELSYS-3].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Larotrectinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for NTRK Fusion-Positive)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion positive

trial_data.efficacy: High ORR demonstrated across tumor types for NTRK fusion-positive tumors.

tier_1_regimens.notes: NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions occur uncommonly (<1%) across subtypes of melanoma. Larotrectinib has shown activity in NTRK fusion-positive tumors. Listed as useful in certain circumstances for this rare molecular subtype [MELSYS-2].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Entrectinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for NTRK or ROS1 Fusion-Positive)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, NTRK1/2/3 or ROS1 gene fusion positive

biomarkers.marker: NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion or ROS1 gene fusion

trial_data.efficacy: Safety and antitumor activity demonstrated in NTRK/ROS1/ALK fusion-positive solid tumors.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is a Pan-TRK, ROS1, and ALK inhibitor. Listed as useful in certain circumstances for patients with NTRK fusion-positive or ROS1 gene fusion-positive melanoma [MELSYS-2].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Crizotinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for ROS1 Fusion-Positive)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, ROS1 gene fusion positive

trial_data.efficacy: Activity in ROS1 gene fusion-positive tumors based on case reports and limited clinical trial data.

tier_1_regimens.notes: ROS1 gene fusions occur uncommonly (<1%) across subtypes of melanoma. Crizotinib is listed as useful in certain circumstances for ROS1 fusion-positive tumors [MELSYS-2].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Trametinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for BRAF Non-V600 or Gene Fusion-Positive)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF non-V600 mutations or BRAF gene fusion

biomarkers.marker: BRAF non-V600 mutations (e.g., L597, K601) or BRAF gene fusion

trial_data.efficacy: BRAF gene fusion-positive tumors and certain BRAF non-V600 mutations (e.g., L597, K601) have shown responses to MEK inhibitors and non-specific RAF inhibitors.
reference_numbers: ME-C 7-8 (Hutchinson KE, et al. Clin Cancer Res 2013;19:6696-6702)
Dankner M, et al. Clin Cancer Res 2018;24:6483-6494

tier_1_regimens.notes: BRAF non-V600 mutations include BRAF L597 and K601, which have shown response to MEK inhibitors and BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations. BRAF gene fusion-positive tumors have also shown responses to MEK inhibitors and non-specific RAF inhibitors [ME-C 2].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cytotoxic Chemotherapy — Temozolomide (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Similar efficacy to dacarbazine. Among cytotoxic agents, temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred [MELSYS-5].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred among cytotoxic agents for patients who are not candidates for further standard therapy [MELSYS-5]. Common cytotoxic agents used in metastatic melanoma include dacarbazine, temozolomide, and paclitaxel with or without carboplatin, demonstrating modest response rates less than 20% [MELSYS-5].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cytotoxic Chemotherapy — Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Modest response rates less than 20%. Among cytotoxic agents, carboplatin/paclitaxel is preferred along with temozolomide [MELSYS-5].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin/paclitaxel was added to the list of cytotoxic agent options in version 2.2026 [MELSYS 2 update]. Multiagent chemotherapy has shown a marginal improvement in response rate with no difference in OS when compared with single-agent dacarbazine [MELSYS-5].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cytotoxic Chemotherapy — Dacarbazine (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Modest response rates less than 20%. No improvement in OS compared to other cytotoxic agents.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Among cytotoxic agents, temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred over dacarbazine [MELSYS-5]. Given the number of effective options available, cytotoxic therapy is not among the preferred options but may be considered for those who have failed or are ineligible for more effective options [MELSYS-3].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Vinblastine/Dacarbazine (CVD) (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Multiagent chemotherapy has shown a marginal improvement in response rate with no difference in OS compared with single-agent dacarbazine.

tier_1_regimens.notes: CVD is listed as useful in certain circumstances (category 2B) among the cytotoxic agent options [MELSYS 2]. Among cytotoxic agents, temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred [MELSYS-5].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: T-VEC (Intralesional) for Unresectable Stage III In-Transit Disease
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable

drugs.name: Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC)
drugs.dose: Intralesional injection, up to 4 mL per lesion

trial_data.efficacy: DRR: 16.3% vs. 2.1% (P < .001). ORR: 26.4% vs. 5.7% (P < .001). CR in 11% vs. <1%. T-VEC associated with a response rate (lasting ≥6 months) of 16%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: T-VEC is category 1 for patients with unresectable stage III in-transit disease based on improved durable and ORR compared to injection with GM-CSF alone [ME-8A]. For patients with low-volume in-transit disease, the high risk of toxicities associated with certain combination regimens may outweigh the benefit [ME-7A, ME-8A]. T-VEC has shown similar efficacy across clinically detected/macroscopic AJCC 8th Edition stage III disease [ME-8A]. Neoadjuvant T-VEC was associated with improved RFS at 2 years (29.5% vs. 16.5%) when followed by surgery versus surgery alone (n = 150) [ME-7A].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Interleukin-2 (IL-2) Intralesional (Stage III In-Transit — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable

trial_data.trial_name: Various phase II and observational studies
trial_data.efficacy: CR in injected lesions may be as high as 70%. 80% for dermal, 73% for subcutaneous lesions.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Intralesional IL-2 is far less toxic than high-dose IV IL-2. Grade 1–2 adverse effects are common but manageable, and grade 3–4 toxicities are extremely rare [ME-8A]. Intralesional IL-2 is usually associated with an injection site inflammatory reaction with local swelling, erythema, pain, and sometimes necrosis [ME-8A]. Listed as useful in certain circumstances when T-VEC is not available [ME-8A].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Isolated Limb Infusion (ILI) with Melphalan-Based Regimen
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable, extremity-only
tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line (for unresectable in-transit disease confined to an extremity)

drugs.name: Melphalan
drugs.dose: Per institutional protocol (typically based on limb volume)

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 73%. CR: 33% (range 26%–44% across studies). PR: 40%. SD: 14%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: ILI is primarily used for patients with limb-only disease with progression on/contraindications to standard therapies. This procedure should only be done at centers with experience with ILI/ILP [ME-8A]. ILI is associated with lower rates of toxicity and morbidity compared with ILP [MS-41]. Addition of papaverine for cutaneous vasodilation has been shown to increase response rate but also the risk of regional toxicity [MS-41].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Isolated Limb Perfusion (ILP) with Melphalan-Based Regimen
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable, extremity-only
tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line (for unresectable in-transit disease confined to an extremity)

trial_data.efficacy: Median ORR: 90% (range 64%–100%). Median CR rate: 58% (range 25%–89%). CR with single-agent melphalan: 47%; with melphalan/actinomycin D: 45%–65%; with melphalan/TNF-alfa: up to 70%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: ILP is technically complex and invasive, making it challenging (or contraindicated) in elderly and frail patients, and difficult to reuse in the same patient in the event of recurrence or progression [MS-41]. A randomized trial comparing hyperthermic ILP with single-agent melphalan to combination melphalan and TNF-alfa did not show a significant difference in response rate [MS-41]. TNF-alfa is currently unavailable for use in the United States [MS-41].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab/Ipilimumab (Second-Line/Subsequent — Preferred for Brain Metastases)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic with brain metastases

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 240 mg Q2W maintenance

trial_data.efficacy: CheckMate 204: Intracranial ORR 50%; intracranial CR 31%. CA209-170 cohort A (asymptomatic, no prior brain Tx): intracranial ORR 57%; intracranial PFS 13.8 months. Nivo1+ipi3 has demonstrated clinically meaningful intracranial activity.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For systemic therapy in patients with asymptomatic brain metastasis not requiring corticosteroids, combination therapy with nivolumab/ipilimumab is preferred in comparison to anti-PD-1 monotherapy or BRAF/MEK inhibitors due to superior intracranial activity [ME-J, SYSCON-1]. Systemic corticosteroids may interfere with the efficacy of nivolumab/ipilimumab and should be avoided in patients being considered for combination nivolumab/ipilimumab [ME-J].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib for Brain Metastases
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — BRAF V600 mutant metastatic melanoma with brain metastases

trial_data.efficacy: Cohort A (asymptomatic, no prior systemic Tx, no prior local brain Tx): intracranial ORR 58%; median intracranial PFS 5.6 months; OS 10.8 months. Cohort D (symptomatic): intracranial ORR 59%; median intracranial PFS 5.5 months; OS 11.5 months.

tier_1_regimens.notes: BRAF/MEK inhibitors result in a high intracranial response rate; however, PFS is shorter than reported data for extracranial disease. Patients treated with this approach are very likely to need subsequent brain-directed therapy and should be monitored closely [ME-J]. Dabrafenib/trametinib combination therapy may provide higher rates of response than single-agent BRAF inhibitor therapy [ME-J].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) for Brain Metastases
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Melanoma brain metastases

trial_data.efficacy: 1-year local tumor control rates from 72% to 100% for patients with limited CNS disease, but lower rates for patients with multiple or large (>2 cm) tumors.
reference_numbers: Shaw 2000 (Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2000;47:291-298)

tier_1_regimens.notes: SRS is the preferred radiation modality for melanoma brain metastases and can be delivered to multiple lesions depending on local experience and technology [ME-J]. Large lesions should be treated with fractionated SRS (3–5 fractions) to decrease the risk of radionecrosis [ME-J]. Adjuvant WBRT is not recommended after SRS/SRT for melanoma brain metastases [ME-J]. Dosing guidelines: lesions ≤20 mm receive up to 24 Gy; lesions 21–30 mm receive up to 18 Gy; lesions 31–40 mm receive up to 15 Gy [ME-H 3].

reference_numbers: Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2000;47:291-298

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT) for Extracranial Oligometastases
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Oligometastatic melanoma

trial_data.efficacy: Higher doses utilizing conformal techniques such as SBRT may offer more durable local control and freedom from regional or distant progression.

tier_1_regimens.notes: SBRT may be considered for selected patients with oligometastasis [ME-H 4]. This potential benefit must be weighed against potential toxicities, and strict adherence to normal tissue constraints is recommended. Spine SBRT regimens include 16–24 Gy in 1 fraction, 20–24 Gy in 2 fractions, 24–27 Gy in 3 fractions, or 25–40 Gy in 5 fractions. SBRT for other body sites include 48–60 Gy in 3 fractions, 40–60 Gy in 4–5 fractions, or 16–24 Gy in 1 fraction [ME-H 4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab + Ipilimumab (Low-Dose) Combination Therapy (Brain Metastases — Alternative First-Line)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — BRAF V600 mutant metastatic melanoma, symptomatic brain metastases, untreated

trial_data.efficacy: Cohort D: intracranial ORR 59%; median intracranial PFS 5.5 months; OS 11.5 months.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For select patients who are symptomatic with BRAF-mutated melanoma who have not been previously treated with a BRAF/MEK inhibitor, BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy results in a high intracranial response rate. PFS is shorter than for extracranial disease. Patients treated with this approach are very likely to need subsequent brain-directed therapy and should be monitored closely [ME-J].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (Adjuvant for Resected Stage IV — Preferred)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Resected stage IV metastatic disease (oligometastatic)

trial_data.efficacy: Nivolumab showed clinically significant improvement in RFS compared to high-dose ipilimumab. For stage IV resected patients, category 1.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for resected stage IV. The NCCN Panel agrees that it is appropriate to extend the indication for adjuvant anti-PD-1 therapy to patients with clinical or macroscopic satellite/in-transit disease and who are at significant risk of recurrence [ADJSYS-1].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab (Adjuvant for Resected Stage IV with Prior Anti-PD-1 — Category 1)
tier_1_regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Resected stage IV metastatic disease with prior anti-PD-1 exposure

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then every 12 weeks for up to 3 years

trial_data.efficacy: Ipilimumab is included as an adjuvant treatment option for patients with resected stage IV disease who have prior exposure to anti-PD-1 agents based on extrapolation of data demonstrating efficacy as adjuvant treatment for resected stage III disease.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for resected nodal recurrence with prior anti-PD-1. Ipilimumab is no longer a recommended first-line adjuvant option for stage III disease at presentation but remains useful for patients who progress during anti-PD-1 therapy with resectable disease [ADJSYS-1]. The recommended dose is 3 mg/kg.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Cutaneous melanoma is a malignant neoplasm arising from melanocytes within the skin. Melanocytes are pigment-producing cells derived from the neural crest (ectoderm) during embryonic development. Cutaneous melanoma most commonly presents as an pigmented skin lesion with irregular borders, color variation, and progressive growth, though amelanotic variants exist. The disease originates in the epidermis (melanoma in situ, stage 0) and may invade through the basement membrane into the dermis (invasive melanoma), subsequently metastasizing via lymphatic and hematogenous routes to regional lymph nodes (stage III) and distant organs (stage IV) [28]. Melanoma is distinguished from other cutaneous malignancies by its propensity for early lymphatic dissemination and its immunogenicity, which underlies the efficacy of modern immune checkpoint inhibitor therapies [592-594]. The current AJCC 8th Edition staging system incorporates tumor thickness (Breslow depth), ulceration status, mitotic rate, sentinel lymph node (SLN) status, number of involved nodes, presence of in-transit/satellite/microsatellite metastases, and serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) for distant disease [28,85,86]. Noncutaneous melanomas arising from mucosal surfaces, the uveal tract, or leptomeninges are considered biologically distinct diseases with different staging, treatment, and prognostic implications and are addressed in separate NCCN Guidelines [36-41]. Clinical subtypes of cutaneous melanoma include low cumulative sun damage (low-CSD) melanomas—typically superficial spreading or nodular subtypes; high cumulative sun damage (high-CSD) melanomas—typically lentigo maligna subtype characterized histologically by marked solar elastosis and lentigo maligna growth pattern; and acral melanomas arising on the soles, palms, or subungual sites [MS-2]. Pure desmoplastic melanoma, defined as ≥90% of invasive melanoma associated with prominent stromal fibrosis, is a histologic variant with distinct biological behavior, including lower sentinel lymph node positivity rates [footnote l, ME-1A].

subtypes.name: Superficial Spreading Melanoma
subtypes.description: The most common histologic subtype of cutaneous melanoma. Characterized by a horizontal (radial) growth phase that precedes vertical invasion. Typically arises on intermittently sun-exposed skin in younger patients. Associated with BRAF V600 mutations and intermittent UV exposure patterns.

subtypes.name: Nodular Melanoma
subtypes.description: An aggressive subtype that often presents with rapid vertical growth from onset, potentially bypassing a prolonged radial growth phase. May present as a pigmented or amelanotic nodule or polypoid lesion. More commonly diagnosed at higher Breslow thickness.

subtypes.name: Lentigo Maligna Melanoma (High-CSD)
subtypes.description: A subtype arising on chronically sun-damaged skin (typically head and neck in elderly patients), characterized histologically by marked solar elastosis and a lentigo maligna growth pattern. The in situ component may extend unpredictably for centimeters beyond visible clinical margins. The in situ component may be referred to as lentigo maligna. Notable for significant interobserver histopathologic variability in labeling of melanocytic neoplasms ranging from moderately dysplastic to thin melanoma [MS-3].
subtypes.frequency: Less common than superficial spreading; predominates on head/neck in elderly

subtypes.name: Acral Lentiginous Melanoma
subtypes.description: Arises on non-hair-bearing skin surfaces including palms, soles, and nailbeds. This subtype is not associated with ultraviolet radiation exposure. Can occur in any racial or ethnic group, and represents a higher proportion of melanomas in individuals with darker skin tones. More frequently harbors KIT mutations compared with low-CSD subtypes.
subtypes.frequency: Most common subtype in individuals with darker skin tones; uncommon overall in white populations

subtypes.name: Desmoplastic Melanoma (Pure and Mixed)
subtypes.description: A histologic variant characterized by prominent stromal fibrosis (desmoplasia) admixed with spindle cell melanoma cells. Pure desmoplastic melanoma is defined as ≥90% desmoplastic component. Often neurotropic and locally aggressive. SLN positivity is less common compared to mixed or conventional subtypes, though this remains controversial due to variability in diagnostic criteria and reporting [266-279, footnote l ME-1A]. More frequently associated with KIT mutations and BRAF wild-type status. Often amelanotic and may be misdiagnosed initially.
key_associations: High local recurrence risk with microsatellitosis

subtypes.name: Amelanotic Melanoma
subtypes.description: Melanoma that lacks significant pigmentation, making clinical diagnosis challenging. May occur across various histologic subtypes. Higher risk of misdiagnosis and delayed diagnosis.
subtypes.frequency: Variable; reported in up to 15% of melanomas

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Cutaneous melanoma is driven by aberrant activation of signaling pathways controlling cell proliferation, survival, and differentiation. The mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway is the most commonly dysregulated pathway in cutaneous melanoma [146-148]. BRAF mutations, occurring in approximately 40%-50% of cutaneous melanomas, are the most frequent driver alterations. BRAF encodes a serine/threonine kinase that activates the MAPK pathway; activating mutations at codon 600 lead to constitutive kinase activity and unrestrained cell growth [146,44]. BRAF V600E accounts for approximately 80% of BRAF mutations, V600K for approximately 15%, and V600R/M/D/G for approximately 5% [44,ME-C2]. BRAF mutations are more common in melanomas arising on intermittently sun-exposed skin, in younger patients, and with truncal location [43]. BRAF mutations outside codon 600, including non-V600 mutations and BRAF gene fusions, occur in approximately 5% of melanomas [ME-C2]. BRAF gene fusion-positive tumors have shown responses to MEK inhibitors and non-specific RAF inhibitors [ME-C2]. NRAS mutations, encoding a GTPase that activates MAPK and other signaling pathways, are present in approximately 15% of melanomas across all sun-exposure patterns and anatomic sites [43, ME-C2]. NRAS mutations correlate with poor survival in both localized and advanced disease [60]. KIT (proto-oncogene c-KIT) encodes a receptor tyrosine kinase; activating mutations are present in approximately 10%-15% of melanomas arising on mucosal surfaces (vulvovaginal, anorectal, sinonasal) and acral sites, and in 2%-3% of chronically sun-exposed skin melanomas, but are extremely rare in melanomas arising on intermittently sun-exposed skin [38,54, ME-C2]. KIT mutations across multiple hotspots show differential sensitivity to KIT inhibitors; exon 11 and 13 mutations (e.g., W557R, V559D, L576P, K642E) demonstrate high sensitivity to imatinib, while exon 17 mutations (e.g., D816H) show minimal sensitivity [55-58, ME-C2]. Other uncommon genomic drivers detectable by multigene panel testing (MGPT) include gene fusions in NTRK1/2/3, ALK, and ROS1, each occurring in less than 1% of melanomas across subtypes [ME-C2]. Germline predisposition genes associated with familial melanoma include CDKN2A, CDK4, MC1R, BAP1 (especially for uveal melanoma), TERT, POT1, and MITF; additional cancer predisposition genes with increased melanoma risk include TP53, PTEN, CHEK2, BRCA1/2, BLM, and ATM [6,15-17,66,67, ME-A1]. PTEN loss of function is associated with melanoma pathogenesis [ME-A1]. Tumor mutational burden (TMB) appears to correlate with response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, with higher mutation burden potentially increasing neoantigen production and immunogenicity [72-74, ME-C4]. Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) has shown potential utility in melanoma; detectable ctDNA following surgical resection of stage III/IV melanoma correlates with worse prognosis in adjuvant studies, though most patients have no detectable ctDNA postoperatively [ME-C3].

epidemiology.incidence: In 2026, an estimated 112,000 patients will be diagnosed with cutaneous melanoma in the United States [1]. Melanoma incidence has steadily increased over the past several decades, though this increase has largely been driven by diagnoses of melanomas in situ and early-stage (T1) melanomas, raising concerns for potential histopathologic overinterpretation [2]. However, the thickest (T4) melanomas continue to rise in incidence, particularly in individuals with lower socioeconomic status and/or skin of color [2].
epidemiology.mortality: An estimated 8,510 patients will die from melanoma in the United States in 2026 [1].
epidemiology.trends: Melanoma incidence has been increasing over several decades. The increase is largely attributable to detection of melanomas in situ and T1 melanomas, though T4 melanomas continue to rise, disproportionately affecting individuals with lower socioeconomic status and skin of color [1,2]. With the advent of effective systemic therapies beginning in the early 2010s, long-term remission is now possible for approximately 50% of patients with unresectable stage III and stage IV disease [MS-2].
epidemiology.demographics: Melanoma occurs most often in non-Hispanic white individuals with lighter skin tones, but can occur in people with darker skin tones from any racial or ethnic group, and in areas without substantial sun exposure (e.g., palms, soles, nails) [MS-2]. Males have higher incidence than females [1]. Incidence increases with age, with median age at diagnosis typically older than 50 years [1, MS-2]. Veterans represent a population with increased melanoma risk, including acral melanoma related to Agent Orange exposure, and are more likely to present with advanced-stage disease [78-84].
epidemiology.source: Siegel RL et al. CA Cancer J Clin 2026 [1]; Chen ML et al. JAMA Oncol 2022 [2]

risk_factors.detail: UV-R exposure is believed to be related to approximately 90% of melanomas in white individuals [MS-6]. Includes both natural sunlight and artificial tanning bed exposure. Intermittent intense sun exposure and sunburn history are associated with superficial spreading and nodular melanoma subtypes; chronic sun exposure is associated with lentigo maligna and desmoplastic subtypes [MS-2,7]. Tanning bed use has significantly increased melanoma risk worldwide, with approximately 2.85-fold higher risk in users, showing a dose-response relationship [4,19,20,70].
risk_factors.strength: Strong (approximately 90% attributable fraction in white individuals)

risk_factors.detail: Population-based studies show approximately 2.85-fold higher risk of melanoma in tanning bed users [70]. Risk increases with number of exposures and years of use [19,20]. Earlier age at first use is associated with earlier onset of disease [20]. Molecular analyses show nearly 2-fold increased mutation burden of melanocytes in tanning bed users compared to non-users [70].

≥5 blistering sunburns between age 15 and 20 years is associated with increased risk of superficial spreading and nodular melanoma subtypes [7,23]. Sunburn history plays an established role as a melanoma risk factor [6,23].

Risk factors include atypical moles/dysplastic nevi (serving as risk markers for melanoma developing elsewhere), increased mole count (particularly large nevi), difficulty tanning, light skin tone/Fitzpatrick skin type I, red hair, blue eyes, high tendency to develop freckles, and pheomelanin-predominant phenotype [3,6,52,53, MS-3]. Atypical/dysplastic nevi are larger, with more color variation than typical nevi, and share some clinical features with melanoma [3].
Male sex is a recognized risk factor for melanoma development [1, MS-3]. Males also have higher mortality from melanoma.

Age greater than 50 years is associated with increased melanoma risk [1, MS-3]. Older age is associated with higher melanoma-specific mortality despite lower rates of sentinel lymph node positivity [239].

Previous cutaneous melanoma places an individual at high risk for developing additional primary melanomas. Approximately 5% of patients without a family history develop subsequent melanomas, compared to approximately 10% of those with a family history [62-64, MS-5].

risk_factors.factor: Personal history of non-melanoma skin cancers
risk_factors.detail: Patients with basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma have approximately 17-fold increased relative risk for developing melanoma compared to control populations [55].
Childhood cancer survivors have a 2.5-fold increased risk of developing melanoma, with a median latency of 21 years from initial diagnosis [10].

Solid organ transplant recipients have significantly increased melanoma risk, with higher melanoma-specific mortality compared to non-recipients [11,58]. Hematopoietic cell transplant recipients have increased melanoma risk [12]. HIV/AIDS is associated with significantly increased melanoma risk [13]. Hematologic malignancies (chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma) also increase risk [8,9].

Family history of melanoma increases melanoma risk. Family or personal history of ≥2 invasive cutaneous melanomas warrants genetic counseling consideration. Approximately 10% of melanomas can be linked to hereditary factors including germline pathogenic variants [61, MS-5].

risk_factors.factor: Germline pathogenic variants (CDKN2A, CDK4, BAP1, others)
risk_factors.detail: Germline pathogenic variants predisposing to familial melanoma include CDKN2A, CDK4, MC1R, BAP1 (especially for uveal melanoma), TERT, POT1, and MITF. Other cancer predisposition genes with increased melanoma risk include TP53, PTEN, CHEK2, BRCA1/2, BLM, and ATM [6,15-17,66,67, ME-A1]. Patients with known high-risk PV in the family (especially CDKN2A, BAP1, or CDK4) should receive genetic counseling [3,17, MS-5].
risk_factors.strength: Strong for CDKN2A; variable for other genes

risk_factors.detail: Residence in sunnier climates or at latitudes nearer to the equator is associated with increased melanoma incidence, particularly in individuals with lighter skin tones [20,21,71].

Male firefighters have increased melanoma risk (adjusted odds ratio 1.56; stronger association [aOR 1.87] in those diagnosed <50 years). Risk associated with exposure to carcinogens in retardants and combustion by-products [72-76].
A rare genetic skin disorder preventing DNA repair from sun damage, leading to UV light sensitivity in approximately 50% of patients. Melanoma reported in 5% of XP patients [14].

protective_factors: Regular sunscreen use may diminish the incidence of subsequent melanoma [790]

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Cutaneous melanoma most commonly presents as a pigmented skin lesion that may exhibit changes in size, shape, color, or elevation. Classic presentations include a new or changing pigmented lesion with asymmetry, border irregularity, color variation (multiple shades of brown, black, red, white, or blue), diameter greater than 6 mm, and evolution over time (the ABCDE criteria). Lesions may be asymptomatic or pruritic, and some patients report bleeding or crusting. Melanoma can arise de novo (the majority, >80%–90%) or from pre-existing nevi. Clinical subtypes include superficial spreading melanoma (most common subtype in lighter-skinned populations), nodular melanoma (which may present as a rapidly growing, amelanotic, or ulcerated dome-shaped nodule and accounts for a disproportionate number of advanced-stage diagnoses), lentigo maligna (typically occurring on chronically sun-damaged skin of the head and neck in older individuals, presenting as a large, slowly enlarging, irregularly pigmented macule), and acral lentiginous melanoma (occurring on palms, soles, or subungual sites, representing the most common subtype in individuals with darker skin tones) [1,2,3,6,28,36,37]. The lesion is most often identified by the patient or during routine skin examination by a clinician. Risk factors include older age (typically >50 years), male sex, phenotypic predisposition (atypical/dysplastic nevi, high nevus count, fair skin, red hair, blue eyes, tendency to sunburn, Fitzpatrick skin type I), personal history of melanoma or non-melanoma skin cancer, family history of melanoma, immunosuppression, prior radiation exposure, and ultraviolet radiation exposure including indoor tanning [1,3,6,7,11-14,19,20,26,27,44,45,61,62,65,68-70].

symptoms.name: New or changing pigmented lesion
symptoms.detail: A new mole or a change in an existing mole in size, shape, color, or elevation; the ABCDE criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution) guide clinical suspicion
symptoms.frequency: Most common presentation; majority of melanomas are identified by patient self-detection or clinical examination

symptoms.name: Itching or bleeding
symptoms.detail: Pruritus, bleeding, crusting, or ulceration of a pigmented lesion; these features may indicate a more advanced or ulcerated melanoma
symptoms.frequency: Variable; reported in a subset of patients

symptoms.name: Palpable lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Enlarged, firm, non-tender regional lymph nodes may indicate nodal metastasis (stage III disease); regional nodal basin corresponds to the anatomical location of the primary tumor
symptoms.frequency: Clinical stage III disease; approximately 15%–20% of patients present with clinically evident nodal disease

symptoms.name: Symptoms of metastatic disease
symptoms.detail: Weight loss, fatigue, bone pain (skeletal metastases), headaches or neurological symptoms (brain metastases), dyspnea or cough (pulmonary metastases), abdominal pain or jaundice (visceral metastases)
symptoms.frequency: Stage IV disease; historically <10% five-year survival, now improved with modern therapies [31-34]

symptoms.name: Subungual or acral presentation
symptoms.detail: A pigmented band on a nail, a dark lesion on the palm or sole, or ulceration/bleeding of a digit; may be mistaken for trauma or fungal infection
symptoms.frequency: Less common overall but represents the most common melanoma subtype in patients with darker skin tones [36,37,84]

signs.name: Asymmetric pigmented lesion
signs.detail: Irregular shape with one half different from the other; may have variegated color (multiple shades of brown, black, red, white, blue)
signs.frequency: Present in the majority of cutaneous melanomas; characteristic of superficial spreading and other subtypes

signs.name: Irregular borders
signs.detail: Borders that are notched, scalloped, or poorly defined; may appear to blend into surrounding skin, particularly in lentigo maligna subtype

signs.name: Diameter >6 mm
signs.detail: Most melanomas are greater than 6 mm in diameter at diagnosis, though thinner melanomas may be smaller; nodular melanoma may be smaller but typically >6 mm
signs.frequency: Present in the majority of diagnosed melanomas

signs.name: Elevation or nodularity
signs.detail: A raised, palpable component suggests vertical growth phase; nodular melanoma may present as a dome-shaped, polypoid, or pedunculated nodule
signs.frequency: More common in thicker melanomas and nodular subtype

signs.name: Satellite or in-transit metastases
signs.detail: Palpable dermal or subcutaneous nodules within 2 cm (satellite) or between 2 cm from the primary tumor and the regional nodal basin (in-transit); biologically and prognostically similar
signs.frequency: Stage III clinical satellite/in-transit disease; uncommon at initial presentation

atypical_or_missed: Amelanotic melanoma: lesions that lack pigment and may appear pink, red, skin-colored, or pearly; can be mistaken for basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, pyogenic granuloma, or dermatofibroma

Desmoplastic melanoma: a variant with prominent stromal fibrosis that often presents as a firm, flesh-colored, non-pigmented papule or plaque, frequently on the head and neck of older males; may be mistaken for a scar or dermatofibroma

Melanoma in situ (lentigo maligna type) on sun-damaged facial skin: may be clinically subtle with poor border delineation, particularly on the face, and may be biopsied inadequately or mistaken for a solar lentigo or seborrheic keratosis

Nail unit melanoma: presents as longitudinal melanonychia (brown-black band on the nail) or a subungual nodule; often delayed in diagnosis, especially in patients with darker skin tones where longitudinal melanonychia is common
Pediatric melanoma: may present atypically, including non-pigmented or partially pigmented lesions, and is sometimes associated with large congenital melanocytic nevi
Intraocular melanoma with extraocular extension: can present with cutaneous manifestations that may mimic primary cutaneous melanoma
Regression or halo features: partial regression may produce white or pink areas within a pigmented lesion that obscure the diagnosis

red_flags: Any new, changing, or atypical pigmented lesion meeting ABCDE criteria requires urgent biopsy
Rapidly growing, ulcerated, or bleeding pigmented lesion (especially if nodular in appearance) suggesting high-risk or advanced disease
Palpable, firm, non-tender regional lymphadenopathy suggesting nodal metastasis (stage III disease)
Neurological symptoms (headaches, seizures, focal deficits, altered mental status) suggesting possible brain metastases
Symptoms or signs suggesting visceral metastatic disease: unexplained weight loss, bone pain, dyspnea, cough, jaundice, or abdominal distention
New or changing pigmented lesion in a patient with a history of melanoma, dysplastic nevi syndrome, or familial melanoma syndrome (e.g., CDKN2A mutation carriers)
Melanoma presenting on unusual sites (nail unit, mucosal surfaces, sole of foot) that may be confused with benign conditions, leading to diagnostic delay
Pigmented lesion in a patient with immunosuppression (organ transplant, HIV/AIDS, hematologic malignancy) at increased risk for aggressive melanoma [11,12,13,44,45]

diagnostic.rationale: Optimal method for histopathologic diagnosis and microstaging; excisional/complete biopsy with 1- to 3-mm margins is preferred to preserve architecture for accurate Breslow thickness measurement
diagnostic.key_findings: Breslow thickness (reported to nearest 0.1 mm), ulceration (present or absent), dermal mitotic rate (#/mm²), deep and peripheral margin status, microsatellites, lymphovascular invasion, neurotropism, histologic subtype, and pure desmoplasia if present [ME-B, ME-1A]

diagnostic.rationale: Acceptable alternative when excisional biopsy is not feasible (e.g., face, nail unit, digit, ear, or very large lesions); full-thickness biopsy of the clinically thickest or most atypical portion of the lesion
diagnostic.key_findings: Same microstaging parameters as excisional biopsy; may be limited if specimen is inadequate for complete assessment

diagnostic.rationale: Generally discouraged as it may compromise Breslow thickness measurement; acceptable when index of suspicion is low or for superficial assessment; broad shave may be acceptable for melanoma in situ, lentigo maligna type
diagnostic.key_findings: If transected at the base, a deeper biopsy (punch or elliptical) should be performed immediately in a separate container [ME-B]

diagnostic.test: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of enlarged lymph node
diagnostic.rationale: High sensitivity and specificity for detecting melanoma in enlarged or suspicious lymph nodes; core biopsy preferred for initial staging of clinically positive nodes to allow neoadjuvant therapy planning [280-282]
diagnostic.key_findings: Melanoma cells with immunohistochemical confirmation (S-100, SOX-10, Melan-A, HMB-45)

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred for tissue acquisition from metastatic sites to provide sufficient tissue for molecular testing (BRAF, KIT); preferred over cytology for mutational analysis [ME-9 footnote ggg]
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic confirmation of melanoma; tissue for molecular/biomarker testing

staging.test: History and physical (H&P) with complete skin examination
staging.rationale: Essential for all patients; includes thorough examination of locoregional area, draining lymph nodes, and complete skin survey to identify satellite/in-transit lesions, additional primaries, or other suspicious lesions [ME-1]
Cross-sectional imaging to evaluate for distant metastatic disease; preferred modality for baseline staging in patients with stage III or IV disease; contrast-enhanced CT [ME-D]
staging.indication: Stage III clinically node-positive patients (baseline staging); stage IV disease; evaluation of specific signs or symptoms in any stage

staging.rationale: May be more sensitive than CT alone for detecting distant metastases, particularly in extremities and soft tissue; can further characterize indeterminate lesions found on CT; superior to CT for detecting distant metastases in retrospective studies [190-191]
staging.indication: Stage III–IV disease; may impact treatment decisions in up to 30% of patients in some series [189,192]

staging.test: Brain MRI with and without IV contrast
staging.rationale: Preferred modality for CNS imaging; brain metastases are common in advanced melanoma; early detection of asymptomatic brain metastases may improve outcomes; CT should be considered as alternative only in patients with MRI contraindications [ME-D]
staging.indication: Stage IIIC (consider), stage IV, any neurological symptoms; periodical brain MRI may be appropriate for up to 3 years for high-risk stage IIIB–IV patients without prior CNS metastases

staging.test: Neck CT or MRI with contrast
staging.rationale: For evaluation of regional nodal disease in head and neck melanoma
staging.indication: Clinically indicated for head/neck melanomas; may be included in cross-sectional imaging protocol

staging.rationale: Minimally invasive staging procedure to pathologically assess regional nodal basin for micrometastatic disease; provides critical prognostic information and guides adjuvant therapy decisions; false-negative rate up to 20% [ME-F, 130,204-206]
staging.indication: Patients with T1b (Breslow <0.8 mm with ulceration or 0.8–1.0 mm ± ulceration), T2a, T2b, T3a, T3b, T4a, or T4b melanoma with clinically negative nodes; for T1a lesions, discuss and consider SLNB if risk features present (age ≤42, head/neck location, LVI, mitotic rate ≥2/mm²)

staging.rationale: May be used to evaluate equivocal lymph node exam prior to SLNB; not a substitute for SLNB; suspicious findings should be confirmed histologically [ME-D footnote a]
staging.indication: Patients with equivocal regional lymph node physical exam; patients who did not undergo SLNB; surveillance after positive SLNB (MSLT-II, DeCOG protocols)

biomarkers.rationale: Activating BRAF V600 mutations (V600E, V600K, and less commonly V600R/M/D/G) are present in approximately 50% of cutaneous melanomas; identified by FDA-approved tests or CLIA/CAP-certified laboratories; guides eligibility for BRAF-targeted therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib, encorafenib/binimetinib) and clinical trials [ME-C, 146-152,649]
biomarkers.when: Recommended for all patients with stage III disease for whom future BRAF-directed therapy may be an option [ME-C, ME-5A footnote jj]; mandatory for initial presentation with stage IV disease or clinical recurrence when targeted therapy is being considered [ME-9 footnote ggg]; tissue (biopsy of metastasis preferred, or archival material) is always preferred over cytology for molecular testing

biomarkers.rationale: Activating KIT mutations are present in approximately 10%–15% of mucosal and acral melanomas and 2%–3% of chronically sun-damaged skin melanomas; sensitivity to imatinib and nilotinib varies by mutation hotspot (exon 11 and 13 mutations have high sensitivity; exon 17 mutations have minimal sensitivity) [ME-C, 153-155]
biomarkers.when: In appropriate clinical setting when considering imatinib or other KIT inhibitor therapy for metastatic disease; particularly relevant for mucosal, acral, or chronically sun-damaged skin melanomas [ME-C footnote, MELSYS-2]

biomarkers.rationale: NGS-based panels testing multiple genes simultaneously; may identify additional targetable alterations (NRAS, NTRK fusions, ROS1 fusions, BRAF non-V600 mutations) and assess tumor mutational burden (TMB); results may guide eligibility for clinical trials [ME-C]
biomarkers.when: Consider if test results might guide future treatment decisions or eligibility for clinical trials [ME-4, ME-5, ME-9 footnote ggg]; if BRAF single-gene testing is negative, strongly consider MGPT to identify other potential genetic targets

biomarkers.rationale: PD-L1 is a coregulatory molecule; testing is performed by IHC with various antibody clones; however, its utility for guiding clinical decision-making in melanoma is not established [ME-C]
biomarkers.when: Testing for tumor PD-L1 should NOT guide clinical decision-making in melanoma; PD-L1 expression does not exclude patients from anti-PD-1 monotherapy; may be marginally informative when choosing between anti-PD-1 monotherapy and combination ipilimumab/nivolumab (descriptive analyses suggest low PD-L1 may benefit more from combination), but routine use is not recommended [ME-C, MELSYS footnote e, Larkin 2019]

biomarkers.rationale: Tumor-informed and non-tumor-informed assays are under development; detectable ctDNA after surgical resection of stage III/IV melanoma correlates with worse prognosis; role for surveillance, minimal residual disease quantification, and serial response assessment remains investigational [ME-C, ME-10 footnote iii]
biomarkers.when: Investigational; not yet integrated into routine clinical decision-making; further research needed to define clinical utility

biomarkers.test: Gene expression profiling (GEP) for SLNB risk prediction
biomarkers.rationale: The NCCN Melanoma Panel does NOT recommend incorporation of commercially available prognostic GEP tests into melanoma care; the Merlin Assay CP-GEP test showed NPV of 92.9% for predicting SLNB status in T1b/T2a melanomas in a prospective trial; high-risk result in this setting portsends a 3 times higher risk of SLN involvement [ME-2A footnote p, Hieken 2025]
biomarkers.when: May be used in select patients with T1b ± T2a melanoma to support shared decision-making when a patient or provider would decide against SLNB if true risk was <10%; should NOT replace surgical oncology discussion of pathologic staging with SLNB; alternative GEP tests not recommended outside clinical trials [ME-F]

do_not_miss: Complete skin examination at baseline (dermoscopy, total-body photography, sequential digital dermoscopy may aid detection of new primary melanomas, especially in high-mole-count patients) [ME-12]
Assessment of regional lymph node drainage basins by physical examination [ME-1]

Microsatellite assessment on pathology: presence of microsatellites (microscopically identified lymphatic metastasis discontinuous from the primary tumor) upstages to at least N1c and stage IIIB; should be reported if observed on biopsy or wide excision [ME-1A footnote i, ME-4 footnote bb]
Assessment for neurotropism/perineural invasion (pathology reporting of present, absent, or indeterminate) which may guide decisions for further excision or adjuvant RT [ME-1A footnote m, ME-B]
avoid: Routine blood tests (including LDH, CBC, liver function tests) are NOT recommended for patients with melanoma in situ, stage IA, IB, or IIA disease; they have extremely low yield for detecting asymptomatic metastatic disease [ME-2, ME-3]

Routine cross-sectional imaging (CT, PET/CT) to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease is NOT recommended for stage 0, IA–IIA (NED); imaging should be performed only to evaluate specific signs or symptoms [ME-2, ME-3, ME-10, ME-11]
Routine brain MRI is NOT recommended for stage 0, IA–IIA patients (CNS metastases are very rare in these stages) [ME-D]
Do NOT use nodal basin ultrasound as a substitute for SLNB when SLNB is indicated [ME-2 footnote r]

staging.system: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition (2017)

t_stages.description: Primary tumor thickness cannot be assessed (e.g., diagnosis by curettage)
No evidence of primary tumor (e.g., unknown primary or completely regressed melanoma)
Melanoma in situ (not applicable for thickness or ulceration)
≤1 mm thickness; unknown or unspecified ulceration status
<0.8 mm thickness with ulceration; OR 0.8–1.0 mm thickness with or without ulceration
>1.0–2.0 mm thickness; unknown or unspecified ulceration status
>2.0–4.0 mm thickness; unknown or unspecified ulceration status
>4.0 mm thickness; unknown or unspecified ulceration status

n_stages.description: Regional nodes not assessed (e.g., SLNB not performed, regional nodes previously removed for another reason). Exception: When there are no clinically detected regional metastases in a pT1 cM0 melanoma, assign cN0 instead of pNX
No regional metastases detected; no in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases
One tumor-involved node OR in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases with no tumor-involved nodes
One clinically occult (detected by SLNB); no in-transit/satellite/microsatellite
No regional lymph node disease; in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases present
Two or three tumor-involved nodes OR in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases with one tumor-involved node
Two or three clinically occult (detected by SLNB); no in-transit/satellite/microsatellite
Two or three tumor-involved nodes, at least one of which was clinically detected; no in-transit/satellite/microsatellite
One clinically occult or clinically detected tumor-involved node; in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases present

Four or more tumor-involved nodes OR in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases with two or more tumor-involved nodes, or any number of matted nodes without or with in-transit/satellite/microsatellite
Four or more clinically occult (detected by SLNB); no in-transit/satellite/microsatellite
Four or more, at least one of which was clinically detected, OR any number of matted nodes; no in-transit/satellite/microsatellite
Two or more clinically occult or clinically detected tumor-involved nodes AND/OR any number of matted nodes; in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases present

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis to skin, soft tissue including muscle, and/or non-regional lymph node
Distant metastasis to lung with or without M1a sites of disease
Distant metastasis to non-CNS visceral sites with or without M1a or M1b sites of disease
Distant metastasis to CNS with or without M1a, M1b, or M1c sites of disease

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Melanoma in situ; confined to epidermis; excellent prognosis with appropriate treatment
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; wide excision with 0.5–1 cm margins; observation after treatment; SLNB not indicated

stage_groups.criteria: T1a N0 M0 (<0.8 mm thick, no ulceration)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Thin melanoma, very low risk of nodal metastasis (<5%); excellent prognosis
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; wide excision with 1 cm margins; SLNB generally NOT recommended unless inadequate microstaging [ME-2]

stage_groups.criteria: T1b N0 M0 (<0.8 mm thick with ulceration, or 0.8–1.0 mm ± ulceration) OR T2a N0 M0 (>1.0–2.0 mm, no ulceration)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Risk of SLN positivity 5%–10% for T1b (discuss and consider SLNB); higher risk for T2a (offer SLNB); adjuvant therapy with anti-PD-1 approved for pathologic stage IIB/IIC
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; wide excision with 1 cm (T1b) or 1–2 cm (T2a) margins; SLNB discussed/considered for T1b, offered for T2a

stage_groups.criteria: T2b N0 M0 (>1.0–2.0 mm with ulceration) OR T3a N0 M0 (>2.0–4.0 mm, no ulceration)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Moderate-to-high risk of nodal metastasis; SLNB recommended; prognosis depends on thickness, ulceration, and mitotic rate
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; wide excision with 1–2 cm margins; SLNB offered; consider adjuvant systemic therapy for T2b with ulceration

stage_groups.criteria: T3b N0 M0 (>2.0–4.0 mm with ulceration) OR T4a N0 M0 (>4.0 mm, no ulceration)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High risk of recurrence and nodal metastasis; adjuvant systemic therapy (anti-PD-1) approved for pathologic stage IIB/IIC; consider baseline imaging for staging
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with high recurrence risk; wide excision with 2 cm margins; SLNB recommended; adjuvant systemic therapy (anti-PD-1) considered

stage_groups.criteria: T4b N0 M0 (>4.0 mm with ulceration)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very high risk of recurrence; adjuvant systemic therapy (anti-PD-1) approved; consider RT to primary tumor site to reduce local recurrence (category 2B)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with very high recurrence risk; wide excision with 2 cm margins; SLNB recommended; adjuvant systemic therapy; consider RT to primary tumor site

stage_groups.criteria: T1a/b, T2a N1a/N2a M0 (clinically occult nodal metastases with thin/intermediate primary tumor)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Lowest risk stage III; SLN-only disease; in patients with very low tumor volume (SLN deposits <0.3 mm), 5-year MSS is similar to pathologic stage IB; toxicity of adjuvant therapy may outweigh benefit in very low risk IIIA [ME-5A footnote oo]
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 78%–93% depending on specific substaging and SLN tumor burden [28]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Consider adjuvant systemic therapy; observation may be appropriate for very low risk IIIA; CLND no longer required (MSLT-II, DeCOG)

stage_groups.criteria: Various N substages including clinically detected node(s), in-transit/satellite disease, and higher tumor burden nodal disease
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Higher risk stage III; adjuvant systemic therapy strongly considered; neoadjuvant systemic therapy preferred for clinically positive nodes; microsatellitosis upstages to at least IIIB
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Systemic therapy (adjuvant or neoadjuvant); surgery; consider RT to nodal basin in selected patients

stage_groups.criteria: T3b/T4a with any N ≥ N1; T4b with N1a/b/c or N2a/b/c; or N2b/c, N3b/c with various T stages
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-risk stage III; appreciable risk of distant metastases including CNS (11%); consider brain imaging; aggressive adjuvant systemic therapy indicated
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Aggressive adjuvant systemic therapy; surgery; RT as indicated; consider brain MRI surveillance

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Highest risk stage III; poorest prognosis within stage III; aggressive multimodality therapy
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Aggressive adjuvant systemic therapy; surgery; consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy for unresectable disease

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, Any N, M1 (distant metastatic disease to skin/soft tissue [M1a], lung [M1b], non-CNS visceral [M1c], or CNS [M1d])
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastatic disease; prognostic stratification by M substage and LDH level; brain metastases warrant multidisciplinary consultation; improved outcomes with modern systemic therapies achieving durable long-term survival in approximately 50% of patients [35,ME-17]
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Historically <10% [31-34]; significantly improved with immune checkpoint inhibitors and BRAF/MEK targeted therapy [35]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative intent with curative potential in oligometastatic settings; systemic therapy (immune checkpoint inhibitors, BRAF/MEK inhibition, combination therapy); local therapy (surgery, RT, intralesional T-VEC) for oligometastatic disease

staging_pearls: Microsatellitosis (microscopically identified lymphatic metastasis discontinuous from the primary tumor) defines at least N1c and at least stage IIIB disease, regardless of SLN status; SLNB should still be considered as SLN positivity would upstage to N2c, stage IIIC [ME-1A footnote i, ME-4 footnote bb]

In-transit metastases (intralymphatic tumor between 2 cm from the primary and the regional nodal basin) and satellite metastases (within 2 cm of the primary) are biologically and prognostically similar, and both define N1c–N3c depending on associated nodal disease [ME-7A footnote vv]

The 2-cm cutoff between satellite and in-transit metastases is consistent with AJCC staging definitions, but satellite and in-transit lymphatic metastases are biologically and prognostically similar [ME-7A footnote vv]
LDH level is incorporated into M substaging (0 = not elevated, 1 = elevated) and is a surrogate for tumor burden; elevated LDH confers worse prognosis in stage IV [ST-3]
Clinical stage III has only one stage group; pathological staging provides more granular substaging (IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID) [ST-4 footnote *]

The AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition (2017) removed mitotic rate from the T1 category but it remains an important prognostic factor across all thickness categories and should be reported [ME-1A footnote g, ME-B footnote h]
Dermal mitotic rate ≥1/mm² was historically used to upstage T1a to T1b but in the current AJCC 8th edition, T1b is defined by ulceration or thickness 0.8–1.0 mm regardless of mitotic rate [ST-1]

For patients with clinically positive nodes (stage III), the presence of microsatellites in the initial biopsy or wide excision specimen upstages the melanoma to a minimum of stage IIIC [ME-5A footnote ii]

management_principles.overview: The NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous represent a comprehensive approach to the diagnosis, staging, and treatment of cutaneous melanoma. The treatment philosophy is multimodal, integrating surgical, systemic, and radiation therapies depending on disease stage and risk. For localized disease, surgical excision remains the primary curative treatment with defined margin recommendations based on Breslow thickness. Systemic adjuvant therapy with immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1) or BRAF/MEK targeted therapy (for BRAF-mutant tumors) is considered for resected stage IIB/IIC through stage IV disease. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy has emerged as a preferred approach for clinically positive nodal disease, with the NADINA and SWOG S1801 trials demonstrating improved event-free survival with neoadjuvant regimens compared to adjuvant-only approaches [1], [2]. For unresectable or metastatic disease, immune checkpoint inhibitors including anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab) or combination ipilimumab/nivolumab represent preferred first-line options, with BRAF/MEK targeted therapy as an alternative for patients with BRAF V600-mutant tumors [3], [4]. Treatment decisions should be individualized based on patient performance status, comorbidities, tumor burden, BRAF mutation status, and patient preferences through shared decision-making.

by_intent.approach: Surgical excision with defined margins based on Breslow thickness is the primary treatment. SLNB is discussed and offered based on risk of nodal positivity: not recommended for T1a melanomas <0.8 mm without ulceration (SLN positivity <5%), considered for T1b melanomas or T1a with adverse features (5-10% risk), and recommended for stage IB (T2a) or stage II (T2b+) where risk is >10% [5], [6].
