by_intent.patient_group: Resected stage IIB/IIC through stage IV, or recurrent disease after resection
by_intent.approach: Adjuvant systemic therapy options include nivolumab (category 1), pembrolizumab (category 1 for clinically positive nodes), or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600 mutation positive. The choice between anti-PD-1 monotherapy and observation involves shared decision-making weighing recurrence risk against treatment toxicity, including approximately 15% risk of lifelong endocrine dysfunction [7], [8]. Duration is 12 months for all regimens.

by_intent.patient_group: Clinically evident resectable stage III disease with nodal disease or isolated resectable in-transit metastases; resectable oligometastatic stage IV
by_intent.approach: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is preferred for clinically positive nodes. Preferred regimens include ipilimumab/nivolumab (category 1 for clinically positive nodes without prior therapy) or pembrolizumab. Neoadjuvant approaches show improved EFS compared to adjuvant-only strategies (12-month EFS 84% vs 57% for nivolumab/ipilimumab in NADINA; 2-year EFS 72% vs 49% for pembrolizumab in SWOG 1801) [1], [2].

by_intent.patient_group: Unresectable stage III or stage IV metastatic disease
by_intent.approach: First-line preferred options include combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab), all category 1. BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations are category 1 alternatives for BRAF V600-mutant tumors. Combination checkpoint therapy is preferred over monotherapy for patients with good performance status who can tolerate higher toxicity rates (grade 3-5: 54-59% for combination vs 22% for anti-PD-1) [3], [4], [9].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary tumor board consultation is strongly recommended for all patients with melanoma. Specific scenarios requiring MDT discussion include: neoadjuvant therapy consideration for borderline resectable disease, management of brain metastases (neurosurgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology), complex desmoplastic melanomas (dermatopathology consultation), and treatment planning for locally advanced or metastatic disease. In the setting of brain metastases, multidisciplinary evaluation is recommended prior to initiation of treatment [10].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status considerations influence treatment selection throughout the melanoma management algorithm. Patients must have adequate performance status (ECOG 0-1 for clinical trial eligibility) for systemic therapy. Immunotherapy combination with ipilimumab/nivolumab requires careful patient selection given higher toxicity rates; patients with good performance status and absence of autoimmune disease are preferred. For unresectable in-transit disease, patients with poor performance status or limited life expectancy may not be candidates for aggressive systemic therapy, and observation of melanoma in situ may be appropriate for patients with poor functional status and/or limited life expectancy [7], [10].

nodes.criteria: Melanoma in situ confirmed by pathology

options.label: Wide excision
options.recommendation: Surgical excision with 0.5-1 cm margins (category 1)

options.label: Topical imiquimod 5% cream
options.recommendation: For LM subtype when surgery is not performed, or for positive margins after surgical excision (off-label use). In a phase 3 trial of imiquimod vs RT for complex LM, 95% response rates at 6 months with comparable efficacy and safety [11].

options.label: Radiation therapy
options.recommendation: For select patients with LM when surgery is not possible or desired

options.label: Active surveillance
options.recommendation: May be appropriate for patients with poor functional status and/or limited life expectancy

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant systemic therapy indicated for melanoma in situ

management_pathways.title: Stage IA Melanoma (T1a: <0.8 mm, no ulceration)

nodes.criteria: Stage IA, T1a, Breslow <0.8 mm without ulceration, no adverse features. Risk of positive SLN <5%

options.label: Wide excision with 1 cm margin
options.recommendation: Category 1. SLNB is not generally recommended unless significant uncertainty about adequacy of microstaging [5], [6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant systemic therapy for stage I disease

management_pathways.title: Stage IB Melanoma (T1b: <0.8 mm with ulceration or 0.8-1.0 mm ± ulceration)

nodes.criteria: Clinical stage IB, T1b. Risk of positive SLN 5-10%. Includes T1a lesions with Breslow >0.5 mm and adverse features (age ≤42, head/neck location, lymphovascular invasion, mitotic rate ≥2/mm²)

options.label: Wide excision with 1 cm margin with SLNB
options.recommendation: Category 1 for wide excision. Discuss and consider SLNB for these patients [5], [6], [12].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant systemic therapy for stage I disease. If SLNB positive, manage per stage III pathway.

management_pathways.title: Stage IB (T2a) or Stage II (T2b or higher)

nodes.criteria: Clinical stage IB (T2a) or II (T2b+), clinically negative nodes

options.label: Wide excision with SLNB
options.recommendation: Wide excision (1-2 cm margin for T1.01-2.0 mm; 2 cm margin for >2.0 mm). SLNB recommended [5], [6].

options.label: Adjuvant therapy for pathologic stage IIB/IIC
options.recommendation: For pathologic stage IIB/IIC: systemic therapy and/or primary tumor site RT to reduce local recurrence (category 2B). Adjuvant anti-PD-1 (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) is approved. Shared decision-making required, weighing recurrence risk against approximately 15% risk of lifelong endocrine dysfunction [7], [13].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For pathologic stage IIB/IIC: consider adjuvant systemic therapy (anti-PD-1 or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+) and/or RT to primary tumor site for select patients at high risk for local recurrence (T4, desmoplastic histology, neurotropism, close margins, microsatellites) [7], [13].

management_pathways.title: Stage IIIB Microscopic Satellites (No SLN or SLN Negative)

nodes.criteria: Microscopic satellites in biopsy/wide excision specimen from primary lesion. Stage at least IIIB (N1c) regardless of SLNB status

options.label: Wide excision with SLNB
options.recommendation: SLNB should be considered, especially if it will alter disease management decisions [14].

options.label: Consider adjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For stage IIIB disease based on microsatellites alone (without satellite/in-transit or nodal disease), patients demonstrate more favorable survival compared with SLNB-positive patients [15]. Clinical trials are recommended.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Patients with stage IIIB based on microsatellites alone show more favorable survival than SLNB-positive patients. Trial entry criteria may not be directly applicable to this subgroup.

nodes.criteria: Positive SLNB. Pathologic stage IIIA/IIIB/IIIC/IIID. Clinically occult nodal disease

options.label: Adjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Options include nivolumab (category 1 for IIIB/C), pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+. CLND should not be a factor in the decision to use adjuvant therapy [7], [16], [17].

options.label: Active nodal basin ultrasound surveillance
options.recommendation: CLND has no impact on MSS or OS based on MSLT-II and DeCOG trials. Active nodal basin US surveillance is preferred if institutional expertise available [18], [19].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For pathologic stage IIIA (SLN metastasis <0.3 mm), 5-year MSS similar to pathologic stage IB; adjuvant therapy may be omitted. For stage IIIA with SLN deposits ≥0.3 mm or higher substages, adjuvant systemic therapy should be considered [7], [16].

nodes.criteria: Clinically positive node(s) confirmed by core biopsy or FNA

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 1 for initial presentation without prior targeted/immune therapy. Preferred: ipilimumab/nivolumab or pembrolizumab [1], [2].

options.label: Wide excision of primary tumor + TLND
options.recommendation: For resectable disease after neoadjuvant therapy or if neoadjuvant not pursued [20].

options.label: Consider RT to nodal basin
options.recommendation: In selected high-risk patients based on non-response/non-receipt of neoadjuvant therapy and/or extracapsular extension, location, number, and size of involved nodes (category 2B) [21].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After TLND and wide excision: adjuvant systemic therapy options are the same as for SLN-positive stage III. Regional RT may be considered in select high-risk patients.

management_pathways.title: Stage III - Clinical Satellite/In-Transit: Limited Resectable Disease

options.label: Complete excision to clear margins
options.recommendation: No clinical data to support wider surgical margins for satellite/in-transit metastasis; clear histologic margins should be achieved [22].

options.label: T-VEC/intralesional therapy
options.recommendation: Category 2B. T-VEC shows improved durable and overall response rates compared to surgery alone in select patients [22], [23].

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: When systemic therapy is given, a neoadjuvant approach is generally favored [1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After complete excision to clear margins and NED: consider adjuvant systemic therapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+) [7].

management_pathways.title: Stage III - Clinical Satellite/In-Transit: Unresectable/Borderline Resectable Disease

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: For low-volume in-transit disease, high risk of toxicities from combination regimens (nivolumab/ipilimumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) may outweigh benefit [24].

options.label: T-VEC (preferred intralesional)
options.recommendation: Category 1 for injectable lesions. Response rate lasting ≥6 months: 16% in highly selected patients with unresectable stage IIIB-IV melanoma [23].

options.label: Consider RT
options.recommendation: Category 2B for unresectable symptomatic regional recurrences [21].

options.label: ILI/ILP with melphalan-based regimen
options.recommendation: For multiple regional in-transit metastases confined to an extremity. Should only be done at centers with expertise [25].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After NED following local/regional therapy: consider adjuvant systemic therapy or observation [7].

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred for therapy-naïve)
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy preferred for therapy-naïve patients. Options include checkpoint inhibitors or BRAF/MEK targeted therapy [3], [4].

options.label: Metastasis-directed therapy
options.recommendation: Resection, SBRT, or T-VEC/intralesional therapy for accessible lesions [10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If completely resected and NED: consider adjuvant systemic therapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or ipilimumab/nivolumab) [7].

management_pathways.title: Stage IV Metastatic Disease - Widely Disseminated and/or Brain Metastases

nodes.criteria: Widely disseminated disease and/or brain metastases. Systemic therapy (preferred)

options.label: First-line systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Preferred: combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy (all category 1). For BRAF V600+: BRAF/MEK combinations also category 1 [3], [4], [9].

options.label: Consider palliative RT
options.recommendation: For symptomatic extracranial disease. SRS preferred for brain metastases [21], [26].

options.label: Best supportive/palliative care
options.recommendation: See NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care [10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy applies only after complete resection of metastatic disease

nodes.criteria: True scar recurrence defined by presence of in situ and/or radial growth phase abutting the surgical scar. After inadequate initial excision

options.label: Re-excision to appropriate margins ± SLNB
options.recommendation: Re-excision to appropriate margins based on pathologic T-stage of recurrence, with or without lymphatic mapping/SLNB [27].

options.label: Adjuvant treatment based on stage
options.recommendation: Recommendations should be based on pathologic T-stage of recurrence; follow guidelines as in ME-2 or ME-3 [7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Prognosis after re-excision is related to the microstaging of the recurrence, treated as a new primary.

nodes.criteria: Local satellite/in-transit recurrence lacking in situ or radial growth phase, with deep dermal or subcutaneous fat recurrence within or adjacent to the melanoma scar

options.label: Complete excision to clear margins
options.recommendation: For limited resectable disease. SLNB may be considered on individual basis (category 2B) [22].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For limited resectable disease: systemic therapy, T-VEC/intralesional therapy, or neoadjuvant systemic therapy [24].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After complete excision to clear margins and NED: consider adjuvant systemic therapy or observation [7].

options.label: Excision + TLND/CLND
options.recommendation: For resectable disease. Excise recurrence and perform TLND if incomplete or no prior lymph node dissection [20].

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred for unresectable/systemic disease)
options.recommendation: For unresectable disease or systemic disease [3], [4].

options.label: Palliative RT
options.recommendation: For unresectable disease [21].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After complete resection: adjuvant systemic therapy options same as for stage III disease. Consider RT to nodal basin in selected high-risk patients [7], [21].

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Workup and treatment options similar to initial presentation with stage IV metastatic disease [3], [4].

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: For very poor performance status after multiple prior lines [10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment is palliative; no adjuvant therapy concept applies

items.text: Detailed personal and family history including prior melanoma, dysplastic nevi, and sun exposure history
items.recommendation: Required for all patients at initial presentation [5], [28].

items.text: Complete physical examination with emphasis on locoregional area and lymph node drainage basin(s)
items.recommendation: Required for all patients with invasive melanoma [5], [28].
Recommended for all patients with newly diagnosed melanoma to identify additional primary melanomas [5], [28].
ECOG performance status assessment for treatment planning [10].

items.text: Excisional biopsy (preferred) with 1-3 mm margins for suspicious pigmented lesions
items.recommendation: Preferred approach. Avoid wider margins to permit accurate subsequent lymphatic mapping. Orientation planned for definitive treatment [29].

items.text: Full-thickness incisional or punch biopsy acceptable for specific anatomical sites (face, palm, sole, digit, ear, subungual) or very large lesions
items.recommendation: Acceptable alternative when excisional biopsy not feasible [29].

items.text: Pathology report: Breslow thickness, ulceration, mitotic rate (#/mm²), deep and peripheral margin status, microsatellites, pure desmoplasia, Clark level (for nonulcerated lesions ≤1.0 mm), lymphovascular invasion, neurotropism
items.recommendation: Minimum elements for pathology report per ICCR and CAP standards [30].

items.text: Immunohistochemistry (HMB-45, Melan-A, S100, SOX-10) for diagnosis confirmation
items.recommendation: Appropriate IHC stains may aid in histopathologic diagnosis [29].

Recommended for stage III melanoma for whom future BRAF-directed therapy may be an option. For stage IV, obtain tissue to ascertain BRAF alterations [31]. Molecular analysis for BRAF or MGPT of the primary lesion is NOT recommended for resected stage I-II unless required for clinical trial participation [5].
In the appropriate clinical setting (mucosal or acral melanoma), obtain tissue for KIT alterations [31].
Consider MGPT if test results might guide treatment decisions or clinical trial eligibility [31].
Testing for tumor PD-L1 should NOT guide clinical decision-making. The utility of this biomarker requires further investigation [31].

items.text: Nodal basin ultrasound for equivocal regional lymph node physical exam
items.recommendation: Nodal basin US is NOT a substitute for SLNB. Consider prior to SLNB for equivocal exam [5], [32].

items.text: Cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast ± PET/CT)
items.recommendation: Not recommended for routine staging of stage 0, IA-IB, or IIA disease. Consider for stage IB (T2a) or higher if clinically indicated. Recommended for stage IIIB/C/D and stage IV [5], [32].

items.text: Brain MRI with and without IV contrast
items.recommendation: Preferred for CNS imaging. Consider for stage IIIC+ disease (appreciable risk of CNS recurrence) and mandatory for stage IV [5], [10], [32].
Recommended at diagnosis of stage IV disease for prognostic value [10].
Encouraged for all patients with stage III or IV melanoma [10].

Consider referral for >2 invasive cutaneous melanomas, or family/personal history of ≥2 noncutaneous cancers in a patient with melanoma (especially pancreatic, kidney, GI, and/or breast cancer) [5], [33].
Multidisciplinary consultation including experienced dermatopathologist recommended for staging and treatment options when pure desmoplastic melanoma is suspected [30].

surgery.role: Surgical excision is the primary treatment for all stages of cutaneous melanoma and remains the only curative modality for localized disease. Surgery also plays important roles in staging (SLNB), regional control (CLND/TLND), and palliation of metastatic disease.
principles: Wide excision margins are based on measured clinical margins taken at the time of surgery and defined by Breslow thickness [34].
For melanoma in situ: excision with 0.5-1 cm margins. For large/poorly defined MIS or LM subtype, margins >0.5 cm may be necessary [34].
For invasive melanoma ≤1.0 mm: 1 cm margin (category 1). For 1.01-2.0 mm: 1-2 cm margin (category 1). For >2.0 mm: 2 cm margin (category 1) [34].
Surgical margins may be modified to accommodate individual anatomic or functional considerations, though narrower margins may increase risk of margin positivity [34].
Depth of excision into subcutaneous fat may be adequate in anatomic locations where excision to fascia would cause significant morbidity [34].

Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) is not recommended for primary treatment of invasive melanoma when standard clinical margins can be obtained. MMS may be considered selectively for minimally invasive (T1a) melanomas in anatomically constrained areas [34].
Orient biopsy incision for definitive treatment (longitudinally on extremities, parallel to lymphatics) to not interfere with SLNB [29].

procedures.name: Wide Local Excision
procedures.approach: Surgical excision with margins based on Breslow thickness. Complete removal to level of fascia (typically preserved unless involved).
procedures.indication: Primary treatment for all invasive melanomas and definitive treatment for most melanoma in situ
procedures.key_complications: Wound healing complications, scarring, functional/morbidity depending on anatomic location
procedures.operative_mortality: Not reported as a specific metric; generally low for local procedures

procedures.name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)
procedures.approach: Preoperative lymphoscintigraphy with Tc-99m radiotracer injection, intraoperative identification using blue dye (isosulfan blue or methylene blue) and gamma probe. SLN(s) evaluated with serial sectioning and IHC [35], [36].
procedures.indication: Clinical stage I/II melanoma. For T1b or T2a+, discussion and consideration of SLNB. Not recommended for stage IA (T1a <0.8 mm without ulceration) unless significant uncertainty about microstaging [5], [6], [36].
procedures.key_complications: Complication rate ~5-10%. Wound dehiscence/infection, seroma/hematoma, lymphedema (higher risk for groin vs axilla/neck), nerve injury, allergic reactions to blue dye [37], [38].

procedures.name: Completion Lymph Node Dissection (CLND)
procedures.approach: Anatomically complete dissection of involved nodal basin after positive SLNB
procedures.indication: CLND has no proven impact on MSS or OS based on MSLT-II and DeCOG trials. Active nodal basin US surveillance preferred [18], [19]. May be considered in select cases where adherence to surveillance is uncertain or high likelihood of additional positive nodes.
procedures.key_complications: Wound dehiscence/infection, hematoma/seroma, neuropathy, lymphocele, lymphedema (20-50%), wound complications [38], [39].

procedures.name: Therapeutic Lymph Node Dissection (TLND)
procedures.approach: Dissection of involved nodal basin for clinically palpable nodal disease. Head/neck: superficial parotidectomy + neck dissection. Axilla: levels I-III. Groin: inguinofemoral dissection; pelvic LND considered for clinically positive inguinofemoral nodes or Cloquet's node positive [20], [39].
procedures.indication: Clinically positive regional lymph nodes without distant metastases
procedures.key_complications: Same as CLND; 5-year survival 30-50% depending on number of nodes involved, extracapsular extension, and primary tumor features [39].

procedures.name: Metastasectomy
procedures.indication: Selected patients with limited metastatic disease amenable to complete resection

surgery.resectability_criteria: Disease is considered resectable when complete surgical removal with negative margins is technically feasible without unacceptable morbidity. Disease may be technically unresectable (involvement of major neurovascular structures) or clinically unresectable (distant nodal disease) where surgery alone would have minimal clinical benefit. Tumors that were locally advanced and unresectable that have become resectable should be considered for surgical resection [20], [40].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: For patients with borderline resectable or unresectable disease, neoadjuvant systemic therapy may convert borderline/unresectable disease to resectable, reduce surgical morbidity, allow targeted dissection of isolated nodal metastases, and potentially de-escalate planned adjuvant therapy based on pathologic response [1], [41]. Close monitoring of lymph node basin is warranted throughout neoadjuvant therapy with consideration to expedite surgery if needed for progressive disease [1].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy serves multiple roles in melanoma management including definitive treatment of in situ melanoma in medically inoperable patients, adjuvant therapy for high-risk primary tumors (desmoplastic/neurotropic melanoma), adjuvant nodal basin radiation for selected high-risk patients after therapeutic dissection, palliative treatment of symptomatic metastases, and definitive/stereotactic treatment of brain metastases [21], [26].
principles: Adjuvant nodal external beam RT should be delivered using a technique judged optimal by the treating radiation oncologist. IMRT may lower toxicity and should be considered [21].
Image-guided RT (IGRT) should be used where clinically appropriate [21].
Hypofractionated regimens may increase risk for long-term complications [21].

BRAF and/or MEK inhibitors may interact with radiation and can lead to increased toxicity. Consider holding BRAF/MEK inhibitors ≥3 days before and after fractionated RT and ≥1 day before and after SRS [42].
Limited data suggest no clear evidence of consistent adverse interactions between immunotherapy and RT, though abscopal responses have been observed [42], [43].
Adjuvant WBRT is NOT recommended after resection or SRS for melanoma brain metastases due to worse cognitive decline without OS benefit [26].

dose_frameworks.name: Primary Disease - Definitive Therapy for MIS/LM
dose_frameworks.indication: Definitive treatment for MIS/LM-type in medically inoperable patients or when surgical morbidity prohibitive. Regimens include: 64-70 Gy in 32-35 fractions over 6-7 weeks; 50-57.5 Gy in 20-28 fractions over 4-5 weeks; 35 Gy in 5 fractions over 1 week for fields <3 cm; 32 Gy in 4 fractions once per week [21].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT for Primary Site - Desmoplastic Melanoma
dose_frameworks.indication: Select cases of high-risk desmoplastic melanoma based on risk factors for local recurrence (category 2B). Risk factors include T4 melanomas on head/neck with neurotropism, desmoplastic histology, close margins [21].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT for Nodal Basin
dose_frameworks.indication: Selected patients at high risk for nodal recurrence based on non-response/non-receipt of neoadjuvant therapy and/or extracapsular extension, location, number, and size of involved nodes (category 2B) [21].

dose_frameworks.name: SRS for Brain Metastases - Primary Treatment
dose_frameworks.indication: Smaller brain tumors: ≤20 mm max diameter receive up to 24 Gy; 21-30 mm receive up to 18 Gy; 31-40 mm receive up to 15 Gy. Caution for lesions >3 cm; single-fraction not typically recommended for >4 cm [26].

dose_frameworks.name: SRS for Brain Metastases - Adjuvant After Resection
dose_frameworks.indication: Smaller cavities: <4.2 cc receive 20 Gy; 4.2 to <8.0 cc receive 18 Gy; 8.0 to <14.4 cc receive 17 Gy; 14.4 to <20 cc receive 15 Gy; 20 to <30 cc receive 14 Gy; 30 cc to <5 cm receive 12 Gy. Not recommended for cavities >5 cm [26].

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative WBRT
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliative purposes only when SRS/SRT not feasible. Consider HA-WBRT + memantine for better prognosis patients (≥4 months expected survival, no metastases within 5 mm of hippocampi). Standard WBRT 20 Gy in 5 fractions for poor prognosis [26].

approaches.name: Adjuvant RT for Desmoplastic Neurotropic Melanoma
approaches.indication: Select patients with high-risk desmoplastic melanoma based on combination of risk factors for local recurrence including T4, head/neck, neurotropism, close margins, desmoplastic histology (category 2B)
approaches.key_trial: Retrospective studies by Strom et al., Guadagnolo et al. support improved local control with adjuvant RT in desmoplastic melanoma [44], [45].

approaches.name: Adjuvant RT for Nodal Basin Relapse Prevention
approaches.dose_fractionation: 48 Gy in 20 fractions over 4 weeks or other regimens
approaches.indication: Selected patients at high risk for nodal basin relapse based on number, size, location of involved nodes, and/or macroscopic extranodal extension. Benefits must be weighed against toxicities including lymphedema and complications [21].
approaches.key_trial: ANZMTG 01.02/TROG 02.01 (Henderson et al.): adjuvant nodal basin RT significantly reduced lymph node field recurrence (HR 0.54; P = .021) but no RFS or OS improvement [46].
approaches.toxicities: Grade 2-4 skin/subcutaneous tissue toxicities, pain, nerve damage, joint AEs, lymphedema

approaches.name: SRS/SRT for Brain Metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: 15-24 Gy single fraction for smaller tumors; 24-35 Gy in 3-5 fractions for larger tumors
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Hold BRAF/MEK inhibitors ≥1 day before and after SRS
approaches.indication: Preferred radiation modality for melanoma brain metastases. Can be delivered to multiple lesions depending on local experience and technology [26].
approaches.key_trial: RTOG 90-05 dose escalation study established maximum tolerated doses by lesion size [26].

approaches.name: Palliative RT for Symptomatic Extracranial Metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: Varies: 8 Gy in 1 fraction to 50 Gy in 20 fractions. Higher doses/hypofractionated regimens may be more durable [21].
approaches.indication: Palliation of symptomatic metastatic disease including visceral, bone, and CNS metastases
approaches.key_trial: Multiple retrospective series demonstrate up to 60% clinically significant regression in carefully selected patients [47].

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy plays a central role across all stages of cutaneous melanoma beyond stage IA. In the adjuvant setting, anti-PD-1 monotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) and BRAF/MEK targeted therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib) have demonstrated improved recurrence-free survival for resected high-risk disease [7], [8], [48]. Neoadjuvant approaches with checkpoint inhibitors or BRAF/MEK inhibitors are preferred for clinically evident stage III disease [1], [2]. For unresectable or metastatic disease, combination immune checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy represent preferred first-line options with durable responses possible in approximately 50% of patients [3], [4], [9], [49]. BRAF/MEK targeted therapy provides an important alternative for patients with BRAF V600-mutant tumors, particularly those needing rapid response [3], [50].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Nivolumab (category 1) or pembrolizumab (category 1). Shared decision-making required.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Anti-PD-1 therapy approved for clinical or pathologic stage IIB and IIC melanoma based on improved RFS over placebo. Pathologic staging with SLNB strongly recommended prior to adjuvant therapy to enhance risk/benefit discussion [7], [13].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Observation (especially for lower risk stage IIB); dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRAF testing for dabrafenib/trametinib eligibility; no PD-L1 testing required

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant - Resected Stage III (Microscopic Satellite/Sentinel Node Negative)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Observation preferred for most IIIA tumors. Adjuvant systemic therapy options for higher risk
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Patients with stage IIIB based on microsatellites alone demonstrate more favorable survival than SLNB-positive patients. Trial entry criteria may not be directly applicable [15].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+; nivolumab; pembrolizumab
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRAF testing if considering targeted therapy

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant - Resected Stage III (Sentinel Node Positive)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Nivolumab (category 1), pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+ (category 1 for clinically positive nodes)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CLND should not be a factor in the decision to use adjuvant therapy. Anti-PD-1 and BRAF/MEK therapy both show improved RFS [7], [16], [17].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Observation for low-risk stage IIIA (SLN deposit <0.3 mm)

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant - Resected Stage III (Clinically Positive Nodes)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Nivolumab (category 1), pembrolizumab (category 1), or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+ (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 1 for all regimens based on trials including this population [7].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Observation; ipilimumab if prior anti-PD-1 exposure (category 2A)

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: After complete resection of metastatic disease, adjuvant therapy considered based on individual risk [7].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ipilimumab/nivolumab (category 1 for initial presentation without prior therapy) or pembrolizumab
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Improved EFS compared to adjuvant-only: NADINA (84% vs 57% at 12 months); SWOG 1801 (72% vs 49% at 2 years). MPR with ipilimumab/nivolumab associated with >90% 3-year RFS [1], [2], [41].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib) if BRAF V600+ and immunotherapy contraindicated; nivolumab; nivolumab/relatlimab
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRAF testing for targeted therapy option

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab). All category 1.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Combination therapy associated with higher ORR (58% vs 45% for nivolumab monotherapy in CheckMate 067), improved PFS, and reduced need for subsequent therapy, but higher toxicity (grade 3-5: 54-59% vs 22%). Anti-PD-1 monotherapy provides durable responses with lower toxicity [3], [4], [9].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: BRAF/MEK combinations if BRAF V600+: dabrafenib/trametinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib, or encorafenib/binimetinib (all category 1); clinical trial
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRAF V600 testing using FDA-approved test for targeted therapy selection. PD-L1 testing should not guide clinical decision-making [31].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy - Progression After Anti-PD-1
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab) or nivolumab/relatlimab; anti-PD-1 monotherapy; BRAF/MEK if V600+; TIL therapy (lifileucel)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For progression on single-agent anti-PD-1, ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab produce approximately 25-33% and 10-12% response rates respectively. Lifileucel considered for patients with good performance status previously treated with anti-PD-1 and BRAF/MEK [51].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ipilimumab monotherapy; cytotoxic therapy (temozolomide, carboplatin/paclitaxel preferred among cytotoxics); clinical trial; T-VEC ± ipilimumab (category 2B)
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRAF testing if not previously performed; KIT testing if relevant histology

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy - Progression After BRAF/MEK
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab) or nivolumab/relatlimab; anti-PD-1 monotherapy
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Response rates may be lower with rechallenge of same-class agents. Consider agents from different mechanism of action [51].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: BRAF/MEK rechallenge if prior disease control and >3 months since discontinuation; clinical trial; T-VEC ± ipilimumab

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Combination ipilimumab/nivolumab (preferred for asymptomatic, not requiring corticosteroids, no prior therapy). CheckMate 204: 50% intracranial ORR [10], [52].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Superior intracranial activity compared to anti-PD-1 monotherapy or BRAF/MEK inhibitors. Systemic corticosteroids should be avoided if possible for combination therapy [10].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF V600+ (high intracranial response but shorter PFS); anti-PD-1 monotherapy (modest intracranial activity)

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab/Nivolumab Combination (CheckMate 067 regimen)
key_regimens.setting: First-line unresectable/metastatic melanoma; neoadjuvant stage III

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.day: Day 1 every 3 weeks (induction: 4 doses); then 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W maintenance

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-5 AEs: 54-59%. Colitis (8-13%), hepatitis (9-11%), pneumonitis (1-2%), hypophysitis (2%), elevated lipase (10-11%). 40% discontinuation rate due to AEs. ~15% risk of lifelong endocrine dysfunction [9], [53].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab/Relatlimab-rmbw
key_regimens.setting: First-line unresectable/metastatic melanoma; neoadjuvant stage III
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-5 AEs between anti-PD-1 monotherapy and nivo3/ipi1 combination [9].

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line unresectable/metastatic melanoma; adjuvant stage III; neoadjuvant
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-5 treatment-related AEs: 12-17%. Rash, pruritus, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies. Long-term responses possible after discontinuation [49], [54].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.day: Day 1 every 2 weeks; OR 480 mg every 4 weeks

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-5 treatment-related AEs: 22%. Rash, pruritus, fatigue, diarrhea. Median duration of response not reached after 38+ months in first-line setting [4], [55].

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab Monotherapy
key_regimens.setting: Second-line; adjuvant (if prior anti-PD-1 exposure at 3 mg/kg)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-5 irAEs: 14-20%. Colitis, hepatitis, dermatologic toxicity, endocrinopathies. Drug-related deaths reported [56].

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib
key_regimens.setting: First-line BRAF V600-mutant unresectable/metastatic melanoma; adjuvant stage III (BRAF V600+); neoadjuvant (if immunotherapy contraindicated)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-5 AEs: 48-52%. Pyrexia (common, episodic, onset 2-4 weeks), fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, arthralgia. Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma more common with BRAF monotherapy than combination. Hypertension 6-14% [50], [57].

key_regimens.name: Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib

drugs.name: Cobimetinib
drugs.day: Once daily (21 days on/7 days off)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-5 AEs: 75%. Diarrhea (7%), photosensitivity, elevated LFTs (ALT 11%, AST 9%), pyrexia [50], [58].

key_regimens.name: Encorafenib/Binimetinib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-4 AEs: 64%. Myalgia (10%), elevated GGT, hypertension, diarrhea [50].

key_regimens.name: Talimogene Laherparepvec (T-VEC)
key_regimens.setting: Unresectable stage III in-transit disease; injectable metastatic disease

drugs.name: T-VEC
drugs.day: Every 2 weeks until no injectable lesions or maximum response

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Injection site reactions (cellulitis, pain, edema), fatigue, chills, pyrexia, nausea. Grade 3-4: 11% [23].

key_regimens.name: Lifileucel (TIL Therapy)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent therapy after progression on anti-PD-1 and BRAF/MEK (if applicable)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Prolonged cytopenias, infection risk during lymphodepletion. Requires adequate cardiac, pulmonary, and renal function [51].

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab/Nivolumab - Neoadjuvant (NADINA regimen)
key_regimens.setting: Neoadjuvant for resectable stage III disease
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Higher toxicity than adjuvant regimens; MPR rate 59% [1].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For patients receiving neoadjuvant therapy: cross-sectional imaging recommended after 6-12 weeks to exclude residual/metastatic disease and assess for surgical planning. For active systemic therapy: assessment at clinically appropriate intervals (every 2-6 months) [32]. For patients on immune checkpoint inhibitors: first response assessment typically at 12 weeks (first scan), though some responses may develop later [49], [54].
response_logic: Pseudoprogression may occur with immune-based therapy, where initial radiographic or clinically evident increase in tumor size may precede regression. It is reasonable to continue immunotherapy beyond initial progression for an additional 6-10 weeks with short-interval imaging in asymptomatic patients [51].
Continued growth 16 weeks after starting immunotherapy should be considered true progression [51].

For patients on BRAF/MEK targeted therapy: maximum clinical benefit commonly defined as no additional tumor regression on at least 2 consecutive scans taken ≥12 weeks apart. Switch to immune checkpoint therapy may be considered at this point [51].
Radiographic response does not always correlate with pathologic response in neoadjuvant setting [1].
imaging_recommendations: For patients rendered NED by surgery: follow-up imaging per stage-specific surveillance guidelines [32].
For patients receiving systemic therapy: cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast ± brain MRI) every 2-6 months as clinically appropriate [32].
Brain MRI for patients with brain metastases: every 6-8 weeks recommended during treatment [10].
Imaging should be performed with IV contrast unless contraindicated [32].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: For residual/resectable disease after neoadjuvant therapy: surgical resection with pathologic assessment [1].
Major pathologic response (MPR: ≤10% viable tumor) following 2 doses of ipilimumab/nivolumab is associated with >90% 3-year RFS [41].
Pathologic response assessment: residual viable tumor reported at 10% intervals. pCR = complete absence of viable tumor; near-pCR = ≤10% viable tumor; pPR = ≤50% viable tumor in tumor bed [41].

For disease progression on systemic therapy: switch to different mechanism of action. For anti-PD-1 progression: consider ipilimumab/nivolumab combination, BRAF/MEK if V600+, TIL therapy, or clinical trial [51].
